_meta.focus: igneous and metamorphic rocks, REE accessories
_meta.notes: Optical properties at standard 30 micron section thickness

XPL.undulatory: common in metamorphic rocks — diagnostic of crystal-plastic deformation

distinguishing_features: Never alters to sericite (use this to separate from feldspar)
No twinning (use this to separate from plagioclase)
Undulatory extinction = rock has been deformed
Absence of cleavage is key — always conchoidal fracture

PPL.alteration: sericite (fine muscovite), kaolinite — common, often patchy

XPL.twinning: Carlsbad (two large domains) — orthoclase; tartan/cross-hatch grid — microcline

distinguishing_features: Microcline tartan twinning is unmistakable — grid pattern
Carlsbad twin = two halves of grain extinguish at different positions
Perthite = Na-feldspar exsolution lamellae visible as blebs/films
Sericite alteration more pervasive than in quartz

PPL.relief: low to moderate (increases with An content)
PPL.alteration: sericite, epidote, zoisite (more calcic = more alteration)
PPL.habit: tabular, often lath-shaped in volcanic rocks

XPL.twinning: POLYSYNTHETIC — parallel stripes across grain, most diagnostic feature

distinguishing_features: Polysynthetic twinning is definitive — no other common mineral shows this
An content estimation: higher extinction angle, more calcic
Zoning common: concentric compositional shells visible in XPL
More alteration than K-spar in hydrothermal/metamorphic settings

PPL.pleochroism: STRONG — brown/orange when cleavage is visible, pale yellow in birds-eye view
PPL.habit: flaky, platy — books or fish-scale texture

XPL.special: birds-eye extinction when viewed down c-axis — circular extinction pattern

distinguishing_features: Strong brown pleochroism is the fastest identifier
Radiation halos around included zircon grains — look for tiny dark rings
Chloritization (alteration to chlorite) very common in low-grade metamorphism
Apatite inclusions as needles parallel to cleavage in some igneous biotites

XPL.interference_color: 2nd-3rd order BRIGHT — pink, green, orange — much higher than quartz

distinguishing_features: Colorless PPL + very bright XPL colors = muscovite (vs biotite which is brown)
The high birefringence vs quartz/feldspar is instantly recognizable
Sericite = fine-grained muscovite replacing feldspar (alteration product)
Common in pegmatites as large books

PPL.pleochroism: moderate — green perpendicular to c-axis, brownish parallel
PPL.cleavage: TWO DIRECTIONS AT 60/120° — critical for separating from pyroxene

distinguishing_features: 60/120° cleavage angle is the definitive separator from pyroxene (which is ~90°)
Green-brown pleochroism — more pleochroic than pyroxene
Diamond-shaped cross-sections (looking down c-axis) common
Common in diorites, andesites — coexisting with plagioclase

PPL.cleavage: TWO DIRECTIONS AT ~90° — critical diagnostic vs hornblende

distinguishing_features: 90° cleavage is THE key — spend time measuring the angle
Weaker pleochroism than hornblende
Square/octagonal sections when cut perpendicular to c-axis
Uralitization: alteration corona of hornblende/actinolite around augite core
Serpentinization (green mesh cracks) is the most diagnostic feature of former olivine
High relief + no cleavage + high birefringence
Iddingsite alteration (reddish-brown rim) in volcanic olivines
If fully serpentinized, original olivine identified only by pseudomorphic texture

PPL.color: pink-red (almandine), colorless-pale (pyrope, grossular), orange-yellow (spessartine), green (uvarovite, andradite)
PPL.habit: euhedral 12- or 24-faced dodecahedra — distinctive rounded polygons

XPL.interference_color: COMPLETELY BLACK — stays dark at all stage rotations

distinguishing_features: ISOTROPIC (black in XPL at all rotations) is definitive
High relief + distinctive euhedral habit
Pink-red color in PPL for almandine (most common metamorphic garnet)
Fractures/inclusions often show radiating pattern around center

XPL.interference_color: ANOMALOUS — blue, greenish, or Berlin blue colors that shouldn't occur at this birefringence

distinguishing_features: Anomalous interference colors (blue-green in XPL) are nearly unique to epidote group
Pistachio green PPL color is characteristic
High relief in PPL
Common as alteration product of calcic plagioclase in metamorphic rocks

PPL.relief: low to moderate (changes with rotation — high relief flash at extinction)
PPL.cleavage: THREE perfect rhombohedral cleavages — can see multiple orientations
PPL.habit: variable — anhedral in marble, rhombic in veins

XPL.birefringence: VERY HIGH (0.172) — highest of common minerals
XPL.interference_color: Very high order — pearl/pastel/milky — washed-out high-order colors
XPL.twinning: polysynthetic twin lamellae (deformation twins in marble) — different from plagioclase twins

distinguishing_features: Extreme birefringence (twinkling/high relief flash on rotation) is definitive
Three cleavage directions visible in various orientations
Effervescence with HCl in hand sample
In carbonatites: abundant calcite + REE accessories = important deposit type
confusion_minerals: dolomite (very similar, but slightly higher birefringence)

monazite.economic_importance: PRIMARY — light REE ore (La, Ce, Nd, Pr) + Th source. Used for U-Th-Pb geochronology.

PPL.alteration: alteration corona common — secondary REE phosphates at margins

XPL.interference_color: 2nd-3rd order — high colors, may be anomalous due to Th content

distinguishing_features: Yellow-brown PPL color + high relief + small size
Often occurs as inclusions in biotite, garnet, apatite
Radiation damage halos in surrounding mineral (Th-bearing)
CRITICAL: requires EDS/TIMA-X to confirm vs. xenotime or other phosphates
Alteration corona (Th-bearing monazite reacts with fluids) very diagnostic

xenotime.economic_importance: PRIMARY — heavy REE ore (Y, Yb, Er, Ho) + Dy source. Also geochronology.

XPL.birefringence: HIGH (0.077–0.110) — HIGHER than zircon — key distinction
XPL.interference_color: high-order bright colors, brighter than zircon

distinguishing_features: Higher birefringence than zircon is the optical separation (difficult without measurement)
Warmer brown color than zircon in PPL
EDS required to definitively confirm
Often overgrowths on zircon cores in metamorphic rocks

zircon.economic_importance: ZrO2 production, geochronology (U-Pb), contains U/Th/Hf and trace REEs. HfO2 from zircon used in semiconductor gate dielectrics.

PPL.habit: perfect euhedral prisms with pyramidal terminations — characteristic

XPL.special: metamict zircon isotropic (black) — radiation destroyed crystal structure

distinguishing_features: LOOK FOR RADIATION HALOS in surrounding biotite — tiny dark rings around tiny bright grain = zircon
Very high relief is instantly visible
Perfect prismatic habit when euhedral
Metamict (radiation-damaged) zircon goes isotropic — looks like garnet but tiny and in felsic minerals
Often included in biotite, hornblende, garnet

allanite.economic_importance: Light REE (La, Ce, Nd) carrier in granites and pegmatites. Often the dominant REE mineral in granitic rocks.

PPL.color: brown to reddish-brown, sometimes nearly opaque
PPL.alteration: often metamict (isotropic core) with crystalline epidote/zoisite rim

XPL.interference_color: anomalous (epidote-group) — often nearly opaque in thicker sections due to strong absorption
XPL.special: metamict core goes isotropic; crystalline rim may show epidote-type anomalous colors

distinguishing_features: Strong brown pleochroism + epidote-group anomalous colors + often partly opaque
Zoned texture: isotropic (metamict) core + crystalline epidote rim — very distinctive
Radiation halos in surrounding minerals (REE + Th content)
In granites: allanite is THE main REE host before hydrothermal alteration

apatite.economic_importance: REE concentrator (La, Ce), phosphate source. Carbonatite apatites contain 1-5% REE. Also U-Th-Pb and fission track geochronology.

PPL.habit: hexagonal prisms with flat terminations — very characteristic; also needles in biotite

XPL.interference_color: nearly black/dark grey — almost indistinguishable from isotropic

distinguishing_features: High relief in PPL + nearly extinct in XPL = apatite (combination is nearly unique)
Hexagonal cross-sections when cut perpendicular to c-axis
As needles in biotite: tiny long transparent needles parallel to cleavage
In carbonatites: can be very REE-rich (carbonatite-apatite deposits)
Near-zero birefringence at standard thickness is key — don't mistake for isotropic

titanite.economic_importance: REE, U, Th concentrator. TiO2 indicator in oxidized magmas. Geochronology.

PPL.habit: WEDGE/DIAMOND SHAPE (envelope shape) — most distinctive habit of any accessory mineral

distinguishing_features: Wedge/envelope/diamond habit is virtually diagnostic — shape alone usually sufficient
Very high relief + warm yellow-brown PPL + very high birefringence
Common in oxidized granites, diorites as accessory
Indicator of oxygen-rich (oxidized) magmatic conditions

bastnäsite.economic_importance: PRIMARY global REE ore — Mountain Pass (USA), Bayan Obo (China). Light REE (La, Ce, Nd) source.
distinguishing_features: Yellow hexagonal grains in carbonatite or alkaline rock context
Presence of this mineral = potential economic REE deposit
EDS confirmation always needed
Associated with carbonatite assemblage (calcite + pyrochlore + magnetite)

pyrochlore.economic_importance: PRIMARY Nb ore. Also contains Ta, REEs, U, Th. Nb critical for high-strength steel and superconductors. Often contains significant REE.

XPL.interference_color: black (isotropic) when metamict, weak birefringence when fresh
XPL.special: almost always metamict in natural samples

distinguishing_features: Brown isotropic grain in carbonatite context = pyrochlore until proven otherwise
Very high relief
Almost universally metamict (isotropic in XPL)
Occurrence: carbonatites exclusively in most cases
